moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, staking a claim in the center and keeping all options open.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: A single central pawn on d4 controls e5 and c5. White keeps the c-pawn and e-pawn flexible for now.
plans: Follow up with Bg5 to enter the Trompowsky Attack
Control the dark squares in the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the king's knight to its most natural square, attacking the e4 square and preparing kingside development.
plans: Control e4 and prepare ...d5 or ...g6
Develop naturally toward kingside castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Trompowsky Attack! White develops the bishop to g5 before playing Nf3, immediately putting pressure on the f6 knight.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The pawn structure is still fluid. White's plan with Bg5 is to provoke structural weaknesses in Bl.
plans: Pressure the f6 knight — trade it to double Black's pawns, or force it to move
Avoid main-line theory and steer the game into less familiar territory
alternatives: Nf3 — the classical approach, entering the main lines of d4 theory

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black counterattacks the bishop immediately by advancing the knight to e4. This is one of the most principled responses — rather than passively defending, Black forces the bishop to make a decision.
plans: Challenge the Bg5 directly and force it to retreat or clarify
Gain time and prepare ...d5 with a solid center
alternatives: d5 — a solid, classical approach keeping the knight on f6

moveAnnotations.annotation: White retreats the bishop to f4, maintaining it on a strong diagonal where it eyes the b8-h2 line. From f4 the bishop supports e3 and keeps pressure on the c7 and d6 squares.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn changes yet. White is content to develop pieces before committing the pawn structure.
plans: Keep the bishop active on the b8-h2 diagonal
Prepare e3 and Bd3 for a harmonious development
alternatives: Bh4 — keeping the pin on the f6 square, but the knight is already on e4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black establishes a strong central pawn on d5, claiming space and providing a solid foundation. The pawn on e4 is now well-supported by the d5 pawn.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d5 pawn forms a solid central anchor.
plans: Anchor the center with ...d5 and develop naturally
Prepare ...c5 to challenge White's d4 pawn

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays a solid, restrained move with e3, supporting the d4 pawn and opening the diagonal for the light-squared bishop.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's e3-d4 pawn chain is sturdy but modest.
plans: Develop Bd3 aiming at the kingside
Prepare Nf3 and short castling for a solid setup

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black immediately strikes at White's center with ...c5, the most active plan. This challenges the d4 pawn and fights for central space. If White captures, Black recaptures with good piece activity.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c5 pawn challenges d4, creating central tension.
plans: Challenge d4 and open lines for piece development
Prepare ...Nc6 to add pressure on d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to its ideal square, targeting the h7 pawn and the kingside in general.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White maintains the d4-e3 chain and lets Black decide how to resolve the central tension.
plans: Aim the bishop at h7 for future kingside attacks
Complete development with Nf3, O-O, and build pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black reroutes the knight from e4 to d6, where it defends the f5 square and prepares to reroute to f5 — an excellent outpost. The knight on d6 is temporarily awkward but has a clear destination.
plans: Reroute the knight to f5, a powerful outpost
Support the center from d6 and prepare ...Be7 and castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its best square, controlling e5 and d4 while preparing to castle. The Nf3 also supports the d4 pawn, which is under pressure from Black's c5.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The central tension between d4 and c5 remains.
plans: Castle kingside quickly to safeguard the king
Maintain central control and prepare c3 to reinforce d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the second knight to c6, adding pressure on the d4 pawn and preparing to complete kingside development.
plans: Increase pressure on d4 alongside the c5 pawn
Prepare ...Be7 and ...O-O to complete development

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles kingside, getting the king to safety and connecting the rooks. This is an important moment — with pieces well-developed (Bd3, Bf4, Nf3), White can now focus on middlegame plans.
plans: Begin middlegame operations with c3 to solidify the center
Place rooks on e1 and/or d1 for central pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the knight maneuver Ne4-d6-f5, landing on a superb outpost. The knight on f5 is beautifully centralized, pressuring d4 and e3, and it cannot easily be driven away by pawns.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The pawn structure remains in tension. The Nf5 puts additional indirect pressure on e3 and d4, mak.
plans: Use the Nf5 as a permanent outpost to pressure White's center
Complete development with ...Be7 and ...O-O

moveAnnotations.annotation: White reinforces the d4 pawn with c3, creating a rock-solid center. This is an essential positional move — with Black's pieces bearing down on d4, White needs this extra support.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has a c3-d4-e3 pawn chain, extremely solid and hard to break down.
plans: Solidify the center completely
Develop Nbd2 to support the center and prepare e4 if possible
alternatives: Re1 — developing a rook, but d4 needs immediate reinforcement

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays ...e6, solidifying the d5 pawn and preparing to develop the dark-squared bishop to e7. The pawn on e6 also supports the Nf5 indirectly by controlling d5.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d5-e6 pawn chain is solid and controls the center well.
plans: Develop ...Be7 and castle kingside
Look for a break with ...f6 or ...b5 later to activate the position

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to d2, the only route available since c3 blocks Nc3. From d2 the knight supports the center and can reroute to e5 via f3 (if the other knight moves) or push e4 by supportin.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c3-d4-e3 chain is fully supported. White's pieces are harmoniously placed behind the center.
plans: Prepare e4 to open the center when the time is right
Reroute the Nd2 to support e4 or maneuver to b3/c4

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the bishop to e7, a modest but practical square. The bishop supports kingside castling and keeps the position flexible.
plans: Castle kingside immediately
Keep the position solid and look for a favorable moment to strike

moveAnnotations.annotation: White places the queen on c2, a multi-purpose square. The queen supports a future e4 break, adds pressure along the c-file, and connects to the Bd3 to create a battery aimed at h7.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. White is building up piece pressure before committing to pawn breaks.
plans: Create the Qc2 + Bd3 battery targeting h7 after Black castles
Prepare the e4 central break with full piece support

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, completing development and getting the king to safety. Both sides have now castled and the middlegame battle begins.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The central tension (c5 vs d4) and the pawn chains (c3-d4-e3 for White, d5-e6 for Black) define th.
plans: Seek counterplay with ...b5 or ...a5 on the queenside
Maintain the Nf5 outpost and keep the center closed

moveAnnotations.annotation: The 2...d5 Bxf6 Line is an important variation of the Trompowsky Attack. Taking on f6 gives Black doubled pawns but the bishop pair. White plays a solid setup with e3, Bd3, and short castle.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the 2...d5 Bxf6 Line — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
